topic: Macros - syn and quote in Depth

turns.content: Hey team, I'm evaluating our port from C++ to Rust. We have a lot of macros there that generate code; do you think we should write equivalent macros or just use procedural macros with syn and quote? Also, what's the best way to parse DeriveInput and handle named/unnamed fields?

Good question. Porting C++ macros is non-trivial in Rust because of its strong compile-time guarantees via procedural macros. Using syn and quote will give you great control over parsing existing ASTs (like DeriveInput) and generating new code, which aligns with idiomatic Rust. You'll parse the input into a DeriveInput, then use FieldsNamed or FieldsUnnamed to differentiate between struct fields, and quote! for token generation. This is more flexible than raw C++ macros because you can manipulate syntax trees explicitly.
But what if we just generate the same code with serde derive? Why bother with syn & quote?

Ah, good point. If your goal is serialization and deserialization, serde + derive works perfectly for most cases because it's well-optimized. However, if you need custom logic or want to generate code dynamically at compile time (e.g., based on macro arguments), then syn & quote provide more power. For example, if your C++ macros conditionally expand based on some metadata, Rust macros can do that too, but with better type safety and ergonomics thanks to syn's AST.
Okay, so I'll write a macro expanding into struct code. What about span preservation? Why is it important?

Span preservation is crucial because it helps with error reporting and debugging. When you parse a DeriveInput, syn gives you spans for each part of the AST (like field names). If you don't preserve these when generating new code, errors won't point back to the original source, making debugging harder. For example, if your macro generates invalid Rust syntax, having accurate spans will show where in the input it went wrong.
So what's the typical flow: parse DeriveInput → transform → generate?

Exactly. You start by invoking syn::parse2() to convert a TokenStream into a DeriveInput, which is an AST representation of your input struct. Then you can walk through the fields or other parts (like attributes), modify them as needed, and then use quote! to generate new code with preserved spans.
Right, so I'll write a macro that expands into an impl block for serialization. Got any tips on how to handle named vs unnamed fields?

Yes. FieldsNamed and FieldsUnnamed are variants inside DeriveInput.fields. For named fields, you can iterate over .named.fields; for unnamed (tuple struct), use .unnamed.fields. When generating code with quote!(), make sure to respect the field types and names correctly. Also, if your macro accepts attributes or additional arguments, syn lets you parse those too.
